claim: Currier A and Currier B use two structurally distinct homophonic cipher tables mapping a single underlying Latin text, such that the bigram transition matrices of Currier A and B are statistically dissimilar (chi-squared p < 0.01) but both exhibit second-order entropy compatible with Latin (approximately 3.6-4.0 bits per glyph at order-2).
evidence_for: High-confidence existing beliefs (0.923, 0.95, 0.959) converge on two distinct cipher tables for a shared underlying language
Currier A (11,022 words) and B (23,766 words) differ substantially in size, consistent with different scribal cipher assignments
Glyph entropy of 3.8627 bits is compatible with Latin (~4.0 bits), suggesting modest compression from homophones
evidence_against: If the two cipher tables encode different underlying languages rather than the same one, bigram structure would differ for linguistic rather than cipher reasons
Small Currier A corpus (11,022 words) may produce unreliable bigram estimates
Hypothesis 0.737 already supports two different plaintext languages (Latin vs. other), competing with single-language assumption
test: Compute the full glyph bigram frequency matrix for Currier A and Currier B separately; apply chi-squared test of homogeneity on the two matrices; additionally compute second-order conditional entropy H(g_i | g_{i-1}) for each sub-corpus and compare to known Latin bigram entropy.
test_metric: Chi-squared statistic and p-value for bigram matrix homogeneity; second-order conditional entropy for A and B
test_threshold: Confirm two distinct cipher tables if chi-squared p < 0.01; confirm Latin compatibility if second-order entropy for both sub-corpora falls within 3.55-4.05 bits; deny if bigram matrices are statistically homogeneous (p > 0.05)

tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: If A and B bigram structures are significantly different yet both fall within Latin second-order entropy range, this strongly supports two cipher tables on one Latin source, collapsing competing hypotheses about two languages vs. two ciphers.
